en.extract:
The leaf lorikeet (Trichoglossus weberi), also known as the Flores lorikeet or Weber's lorikeet, is a species of parrot that is endemic to the Indonesian island of Flores. It was previously considered a subspecies of the rainbow lorikeet, but following a review in 1997, it is increasingly treated as a separate species.


Description
Unlike all other members of the rainbow lorikeet group, the leaf lorikeet has an overall green plumage, with only a paler lime green chest and collar. With a total length of approximately 23 cm (9 in), it is the smallest member of the rainbow lorikeet group.


Habitat and conservation
It inhabits the edge of primary forest, secondary forest, woodland and plantations at altitudes up to 1,400 m (4,600 ft).  It remains fairly common, but its relatively small distribution could give cause for future concern. As well, its population is believed to be decreasing, with fewer than 20,000 mature individuals believed to be in the wild.


References


External links

Leaf lorikeet. Oriental Bird Images.

The '''leaf lorikeet''' ('''''Trichoglossus weberi'''''), also known as the '''Flores lorikeet''' or '''Weber's lorikeet''', is a species of [[parrot]] that is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Indonesia]]n island of [[Flores]]. It was previously considered a [[subspecies]] of the [[rainbow lorikeet]],<ref name=JuniperParr>Juniper, T., & M. Parr (1998). ''A Guide to the Parrots of the World.'' Pica Press. {{ISBN|1-873403-40-2}}</ref> but following a review in 1997,<ref>[[Richard Schodde|Schodde, R.]] & [[Ian J. Mason|I. J. Mason]] (1997). ''Zoological Catalogue of Australia, Volume 37, Part 2: Aves (Columbidae to Coraciidae).'' Australian Biological Resources Study. {{ISBN|0-643-06037-5}}</ref> it is increasingly treated as a separate species.<ref>Dickinson, E. C. (editor) (2003). ''The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World.'' 3d edition. Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|0-7136-6536-X}}</ref><ref>Gill, F., M. Wright, & D. Donsker (2009). ''[http://www.worldbirdnames.org/ IOC World Bird Names].'' Version 2.1. Accessed 20-06-2009</ref>

== Description ==
Unlike all other members of the rainbow lorikeet group, the leaf lorikeet has an overall green [[plumage]], with only a paler [[lime green]] chest and collar.<ref name=JuniperParr/> With a total length of approximately {{convert|23|cm|in|abbr=on|0}}, it is the smallest member of the rainbow lorikeet group.<ref name=Arndt>[http://www.arndt-verlag.com/projekt/birds_3.cgi?Desc=E013.htm&Pic=013_1.JPG Lexicon of Parrots, online version.] Arndt Verlag. Accessed 20-06-2009</ref>

== Habitat and conservation ==
It inhabits the edge of [[primary forest]], [[secondary forest]], woodland and plantations at altitudes up to {{convert|1400|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Wallacea>Coates, B. J., & K. D. Bishop (1997). ''A Guide to the Birds of Wallacea.'' Dove Publications Pty. Ltd. {{ISBN|0-9590257-3-1}}</ref>  It remains fairly common,<ref name= Wallacea/> but its relatively small distribution could give cause for future concern. As well, its population is believed to be decreasing, with fewer than 20,000 mature individuals believed to be in the wild.<ref>{{Cite web|last=International)|first=BirdLife International (BirdLife|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Trichoglossus weberi|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|access-date=2021-05-12|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref>
